sentences.text: Well, at this point in time, we're in Emalia and working 12-hour shifts.
And I was working on a-- on a-- a machine, and my brother and my father were working on the same floor at the same time.
And my mother was, again, a cleaning lady working in the-- in the laundry.
At that time, there were four of us in Emalia.
Oh, there were about-- Emalia, probably about 600 to 700 people.
Well, Schindler used to, as you-- you've heard, had these parties up in the-- in his office and entertaining and so on.
And at the end of these-- these bouts, he used to come out in the wee hours of the morning at night.
And we worked on this factory floor, and he would come down and visit, sort of walk slowly, walk through, and actually stop at different places and talk to people.
And he would always address us with-- by our names, you know, he-- he remember-- practically remembered everybody by name.
He called me Little Leyson.
He used--
In German.
Kleine [?
Leython. ?]
Absolutely, yes.
He would-- you know--
He was a pretty decent type of human being in the first place.
And so seeing me there, he would stop and talk.
And he would stop and talk to my father, and periodically he would-- occasionally he would leave a half a pack of cigarettes sort of by accident on his workstation.
And at one point-- and one time he stopped at my father's workstation, put his arm around him, on his shoulder, and he said, don't worry.
Everything is going to be OK.
I'm sure he didn't know it was going to be OK.
But it was a sort of a human kind of thing to do, to speak.
No-- no Nazi would ever do a thing like that.
And he'd speak to me and my brother, who also worked on the next machine.
And the-- the following day, I would find out that he ordered a special ration of-- an extra ration of soup for me, something like that.
Just these kind of things.
But eventually the-- so that gives you an idea of what Schindler-- how Schindler was acting in relation to his Jewish workers.
Each--
And my-- specially my family.
And I'm sure that everyone on Schindler's company-- in Schindler's company had a personal story like this to tell, where Schindler acted in a human fashion rather than the Nazi fashion.
And so eventually the war for the Germans went bad in the Eastern Front, and the-- the Russians were advancing, and these companies were going to be liquidated.
My sister's company had actually closed, and all the workers were sent back to Plaszow.
But Schindler decided that he would-- he would not let his-- his workers simply go.
He would-- he was going to transfer his company-- lock, stock, and barrel-- over to Czechoslovakia and move his Jewish workers with him.
And this was a pretty outlandish or preposterous kind of suggestion, really.
All these experts were-- had to be transferred with the company.
But he managed to do all this with-- by bribing people and by his fast talking and whatever.
Not-- not in so many words.
But why he was moving the company was simply so that he would have someplace for us to-- to move to.
In any case, he-- we did move the company.
And-- and the men were then sent back, temporarily, to Plaszow, back to this main camp, and as well as the women.
And a few weeks after that, we were put in a transport and sent to-- on the way to the new company, new location called Brunnlitz.
In Czechoslovakia.
By the way, Czechoslovakia, Brunnlitz is sort of his home area, Schindler's home area.
He-- he originated in Sudeten, Germany.
So, anyway, he-- we went.
The men were sent first, and we went through a camp called Gross Rosen, which was a extermination camp.
The women then followed, and they ended up in Auschwitz.
Well, we were on this special Schindler's list.
There were about 500, 600 men.
No, no.
When this-- this experience going through that camp was absolutely unreal.
I try to recall how long we were there sometimes, and I can't.
I can't tell whether I was there three weeks or three days or three months, you know, it--
It was because we were treated in such inhumane fashion, as if we could have been boxes of baggage of some kind.
No.
But the-- the signs of the killing factories were in place.
Well, the crematoria was visible.
And by that time, we could recognize what those were.
Well, it had the-- a tall chimney coming up and the-- the angular building.
It was obviously a crematorium.
Oh, yes, many times, many times.
We went-- we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to remove our clothes, go through the showers, which were turned out to be real showers, and treated On the other side, we went out.
All our belongings were left on-- on the-- one side.
And we never saw our belongings again.
We were given-- later on, eventually given something to wear, some rags.
Yes.
There were inmates that had been living there some time--
--whose-- no-- whose job it was to-- to do the shaving.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were in line waiting to be shaved, and there were these people standing there doing-- some people shaving the hair and some body hair and so on in different stations.
Yes.
And so they saw me coming up to-- to the-- to be shaved, and I had my hair shaved.
And some-- the next person looked at me and just passed me right up, says, keep going, you know, just move on.
It wasn't anything-- you know, I was just a little kid.
I was undernourished and just skinny, little guy.
So they just pushed me right through.
It was like-- it was like being among cattle.
And it's like, you know, everybody was taller than I was, and it was-- I was just bounced around from-- from one place to another.
Yes, at a small distance.
Yes, uh-uh, I have, but not under these conditions.
Humiliating.
You know, my father had always been a very proud man.
He- he made his living with his hands, and he was proud to be-- to be able to support his family by-- with his skills, and to be humiliated like this.
But he'd been humiliated before.
It was right at the very beginning of the war by these Nazis who, as soon as they came in, they had ended up roughing him up and taking him to jail.
And when he came out we couldn't find him for several months.
And when he came out-- out of that experience-- that was just at the very beginning of the war-- he was never really the same man that-- that he was before.
Yes, I did, uh-huh.
I did.
I had that-- that feeling, but it was a little silly.
No one could protect anybody.
We were all in the same-- and we were all slated to-- to be treated exactly the same.
So there was-- and that's the reason why I think that my parents suffered a lot more than I did.
Because they really-- for parents to take care of the children is-- is-- is the normal thing, and they just could-- they couldn't perform that.
They couldn't do that.
You understand that now as an adult, I guess.
Right, exactly.
Came out of the showers and shaved.
We-- they left us out there in the dark, in the cold.
And-- and the fall of-- of the year in that area gets pretty cold.
For most of the night we're just standing there naked.
And the-- the scene that must have-- if you could-- you can see that, it looked like this-- this-- these-- this constant motion of naked bodies moving.
We were in this group, but I know that it was pretty cold to be outside, especially outside of the group.
So I would work my way in to the middle, where it's a little warmer, where the bodies were.
And if I stood still long enough, it was pretty soon I was outside again, because everybody else was doing the same thing.
And we were just kind of moving around the whole time we were out there.
And eventually, they-- somebody decided to put us in the barracks.
And they-- and we were-- the barracks must have been as the size of this room.
And we were all stashed in like you do chairs, with one nested into the other.
And I just-- as soon as it got warm in the barracks, I just fell sleep, just dead.
And the-- when I woke up, everybody else was asleep.
And the bodies were just mingled all around on the floor, everybody leaning on a-- on each other, and it was just a big mess.
And-- and that--
--and that was--
I never did.
And then we finally got some clothes, these rags.
They gave us some-- somebody else's clothes.
We didn't have the stripes then.
We just-- they were just-- they just gave us something that they have taken away from somebody else and-- and, you know, sorted it out.
And so then we-- we finally-- we were treated like this the whole time.
We were always counted and measured and-- and restricted.
We couldn't go to the bathroom.
And we were given barely enough to survive, just a bowl of soup and some-- and we were always chased.
And somewhere-- we were always having to go someplace and stand and then go somewhere else and stand.
So it was such an unreal situation that, as I say, it was-- I couldn't tell day from night, and I couldn't tell how long I was there.
And finally we were put on these-- on these train-- on this train and sent to Brunnlitz.
But I-- I was never certain.
I was always in doubt that that actually was going to happen.
And we were there in the train for about 24 hours and-- How many of you were there?
Just the men.
There were about 500, 600 of us.
Yes, uh-huh.
We were in the same-- we were in the same car, railroad car.
Yeah.
No, I never saw the list.
But we were called by name and--
--then we just, you know, we went to the railroad, You know.
On the way to the railroad station, we were all lined up and taken into the railroad cars and--
--slammed-- slammed shut.
And the train started up, and we were all standing there.
It was only standing room.
And we stood there until we could stand no longer and just kind of clomped down to the ground and finally ended up in Brunnlitz.
It was an-- kind of an isolated place.
We were really removed from everything-- anything that was familiar.
That was the worst time of my experience and of the experience of those of us who were in this group.
Because we were-- at this point, we were simply completely isolated from anything we knew, like, our Poland, our homes, you know, the-- the atmosphere-- the whole thing.
A different country.
And we had no contact with anything or anybody.
And the food, the rations were very, very low.
It was a small village, small town, and we were outside of the town in a-- in a camp.
And the camp was surrounded, the factory as well as the camp.
And then we just went to work from-- from the barracks into the-- to the factory.
Yes, yeah.
He was there.
Yes, he did.
He was there.
And he told us that, you know, we're in Brunnlitz now, and then these are the barracks and so on.
The SS.
Sure.
The German SS ran the camp.
They had special troops who are-- who were assigned the guarding of the camp.
And they had a commandant, just like [?
Goeth. ?]
Leipold. ?] That's a mean person who was-- who liquidated many ghettos in the East and did-- carried out many atrocities.
A German SS, yes.
Well, towards the end, the orders came down from headquarters that all these people in these camps were to be killed before the-- the-- they left, before the-- the army of liberations came.
In this-- in our case, it was the Russians.
But in-- in other cases were the Allies.
And we were-- he was actually preparing to do just that.
But Schindler somehow talked him out of it.
He-- he-- he had a way.
And he had actually given him some papers to tell him that-- false papers-- that-- that he was needed in Berlin or something, and he-- he left.
The commandant left.
And so we were left with a small group of SS guards, and they were pretty much-- pretty well defeated by that time, you know.
And so--
Not very much.
At this-- by that point-- at that point, Schindler had more control over what was happening in the camp than-- than he did before.
Before, he-- the camps were really controlled by the SS.
Well, in Brunnlitz we were about eight months, and then we-- the-- the Russians came in, and we were liberated there.
We were-- we were-- actually, the factory was reestablished, and we were doing work in the-- in the factory, working 12-hour shifts.
Well, we were, at that point, you know, with-- at the-- in Emalia, we were making pots and pans and war-related things.
And then when we got to-- to Brunnlitz, it was just the war-related product that we were trying to reestablish.
But it never got to the point where anything was shipped.
Did you know that you weren't getting anywhere with your efforts, or that you-- Well, we were going along as if progress was being made.
And I know for a fact that, at one point, there was a lot of pressure on Schindler to start manufacturing and producing.
And he was contemplating purchasing products, similar products, and shipping them as if they were produced in his company.
Well, that was common knowledge among the inmates at that point.
But--
Yes.
And going-- getting back to that.
When my sister was in-- in Plaszow and we were getting ready to-- to go to Brunnlitz on Schindler's list, my father asked Schindler to put my sister on, and he did that.
So my sister Aviva and my brother David and I and my parents all ended up on-- in Schindler's company.
No.
He never asked for anything, only-- only our-- our good-- you know, we-- I-- I-- I like to think that we made an honest person out of Schindler.
You know, he was claiming that we were all skilled people.
You know, we really were.
[LAUGHS] But that's nonsense.
He-- he did that because he couldn't face letting us be murdered.
He-- he did-- he's one person who did not accept the Nazi philosophy, and he was not going to go along with it if he could.
And he spent a lot of his money that he made.
He came to Poland-- he didn't come to Poland to-- to save Jews.
He came to Poland to make his fortune, and he did.
And he-- I'm sure it never occurred to him that he was going to end up doing what he did.
It just happened that circumstances were such that he was faced with this choice.
And being the kind of person he was, he couldn't.
He couldn't go along with it.
And he did a-- he did a wonderful thing.
I mean, that's-- something that's an incredible thing, I should say.
And for that, you know, there were many heroes in-- in this-- in this war.
I would place my parents as in-- in that category as heroes.
They genuinely did these things quietly and were-- and suffered and did whatever they did.
But not only was it thanks to Schindler that we survived, but it was thanks to my parents.
In May, in May-- May 8 or May 9, 1945.
We were in Brunnlitz, and the first--
We-- well, first of all, Schindler told us that the night before or the day before that the war is over and that-- that we were-- we were free.
No one moved, because we were stunned.
We-- we weren't-- it's not like-- it's not like the movies, where now we're free, and everybody just goes off into happy land all of a sudden.
We were-- we were crippled people.
And after spending that much time being humiliated and-- and under Nazi occupation, we were just-- we just didn't know what to do.
We were-- we stayed in the camp for a few weeks until a transport was formed, and we were repatriated.
There was a program of repatriation of people who were moved from one country to another and wanted to return.
So there was a train being formed that was going to Poland from-- from Brunnlitz, and we got on it because--
The whole-- all the Leysons.
And this was the natural thing to do, to go back where we came from.
And we got off the train, and people got off at different-- in different places closest to their home.
And we got off in Cracow, and we were well received by my father's friends from before the war.
He-- he had good friends he worked with.
They were non-Jews.
And they received us and put us up for a few days, you know, in their home and everything.
But Poland was not a very hospitable place to be for Jews.
So inhospitable that people who survived the concentration camps and returned to Poland were actually murdered after the war by the Polish population.
I went to funerals of such a thing.
They had been killed by the-- by the Polish nationals after they had survived the-- the war.
Quite a few, yeah.
Well, my father went back and got-- actually got his job back, the pre-war job, and was going to find out if it's possible to resettle and reestablish his position.
But soon we-- you know, we soon found out that this was not going to be possible.
And so my sister, Aviva, and David went to Czechoslovakia, and we thought maybe we would resettle there.
And so they went sort of as our Leyson scouts to see how this would work.
And in the meantime, the situation in Poland was not-- getting worse, and so we decided to escape from Poland.
So in 1946-- we got back to Poland in 1945-- in 1946, we went-- escaped from Poland and went back to Germany.
Originally, we thought we would-- since my brother and sister were in Czechoslovakia, that we, on the way to Germany, would get off and join them, but that was not possible.
So we ended up in Germany--
--in a displaced persons camp.
In Wetzlar.
Wetzlar is a city near-- near Giessen and near Frankfurt, Frankfurt am Main.
We remained there for three years.
Well, you know, I was a-- a person with-- I was 16 years old, and a third of my life was-- was destroyed.
So-- and education in our family and-- and among Jews in general was pretty high on the list.
And so I started looking for-- for someplace to learn something.
And we were beginning to receive these care packages.
From our family in the United States who left Narewka in the early 1900s when there were still pogroms in-- against Jews.
My brothers-- my-- my mother's brother and my mother's sister went to the United States and established their family.
And they've lived here since the early 1900s.
They assumed that we were dead, at first.
They had heard about what had happened to Narewka people, and they just assumed that we were included.
And then a year or so later, we added our names to the-- to a list of groups of organizations that did searches.
And we told them that we had relatives in the United States and this is what our name was.
And-- and they asked us where they lived, and we said, they live in New York.
You know, who doesn't live in New York?
Anyway, they actually found our relatives and told them that there was a possibility that there are some survivors in Europe who are related.
And it turned out that it was true.
And so they proceeded to send papers out for us so that we can come to the United States.
In the meantime, my sister and my brother David left Czechoslovakia and went to Israel to live.
I always did.
I always had.
Well, I was-- I was with my parents.
And in 1946, I was 16, and I was the only one left with my parents out of all the children.
And it was decided that it might be best if we went to the United States.
And since my brother and sister were in Israel, that, from the United States, we might be able to help them better and so on.
So that was the decision, so I went along with them.
But we were receiving these care packages in the meantime.
And-- and the-- the situation in Germany at the time was not very-- very good.
Food was scarce.
And so we were able to use some of the food that we were receiving to pay for lessons for me.
Well, I was a-- I went to a-- found a German engineer who was out of work because he used to be a Nazi, and I was taking engineering lessons from him.
Ah, but he was a wonderful guy.
I know.
Neu, ?] Wilhelm [?
Neu. ?]
Well, he-- he had a family of five children, just like our family.
And he spoke plainly about what had happened.
You could tell he was not one of those people who-- who would deny knowing about what had happened.
He wasn't one of them.
He-- he-- he said-- he flat out told us that, yes, he joined the-- the Nazi Party, that he was an engineer, and he was in-- in danger of losing his job.
And so--
